settings.title: A Look Inside Our Edit Room
settings.excerpt: The shoot is half the work, the quiet hours afterward at the desk are the other half.
settings.body:
Clients see the camera. What they rarely see is the room where a gallery actually comes together, two days later, with a cold coffee and a careful eye.

Editing at Framelight is not about filters. It is about matching skin tones across a hundred frames, fixing the stray distraction at the edge, and choosing the eight images that tell the story instead of the eighty that merely happened.

What actually happens at the desk

The first pass is the hardest and the most important. We sit with the full set and cut, fast and honestly, down to the frames that earn their place. Only then does the slower work begin, and most of it is the kind you are not supposed to notice.

  • Matching tone and colour so a gallery feels like one afternoon.
  • Clearing the stray sign or passer-by at the edge of a frame.
  • Gentle work on light and contrast, never a heavy filter.

Our retoucher, Yuki, often says the goal is for you to never notice the work. If a photo simply feels like the moment you remember, we did it right.

Why it takes the time it does

A careful edit on a full session runs into hours, not minutes, which is why galleries take a week or two rather than turning up the next morning. We would rather hand you a smaller, stronger set than a folder of everything we shot.

When the gallery lands, you are seeing the survivors of a long, quiet afternoon of decisions. The hope is that none of that shows, and that the photos just feel like the day you remember.
